planting.note: Divide established clumps after flowering in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade; Clivia gardenii produces narrower, darker leaves than C. miniata and nodding (pendant) rather than erect flowers. It is one of the rarer species in cultivation, growing naturally in the moist, shaded, low-altitude forests of KwaZulu-Natal. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; Garden Clivia differs from the commonly grown orange clivia (C. miniata) in producing distinctly different flowers — the individual blooms nod or are pendant (pointing downward or outward) rather than facing upward and outward as in C. miniata, creating a more quietly elegant, drooping display of funnel-shaped flowers in orange-red to yellow-red above the same broad, strap-shaped, deep-green evergreen leaves. The nodding flower form, while less spectacular than the bold C. miniata umbels, has a refined, somewhat mysterious quality — the pendant flowers dangling in clusters that must be lifted to be viewed. It is more shade-tolerant and moisture-loving than C. miniata in its natural habitat of humid, low-altitude KwaZulu-Natal forests near streams and in swampy ground. As a garden plant for zones 9–11, it provides the same outstanding shade-tolerance as C. miniata — evergreen foliage in deep shade, winter flowers — in a different and somewhat more collector-oriented flower form. Rarely seen in general horticulture; sought by clivia specialists.

planting.note: Plant rhizomatous bulbs just below the soil surface in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; paintbrush lily requires consistent moisture during growth (spring through fall) but prefers reduced watering when dormant in winter. The flowers often appear before the leaves in spring. All parts are highly toxic if ingested. Plants resent disturbance — choose a permanent location.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; paintbrush lily is one of the most dramatically beautiful shade-garden bulbs in the world — a South African native of forest margins and shaded kloofs (ravines) producing in spring (often before the leaves emerge) a spectacular, dense, globe-like umbel of 50–100 individual, bright scarlet-red to orange-red flowers on a stout stem, each flower having a slender, tubular perianth with projecting stamens, the aggregate effect being a large, spherical, brilliant-red paintbrush head of exuberant, vivid color. The flower heads are 6–8 inches across — a substantial, tropical-looking display on a moderate-sized plant. After the flowers fade, large, broad, matte-green leaves with prominent veins emerge to provide attractive foliage through summer. Orange-red berries follow the flowers. In zones 9–11, it is an outstanding shade-garden perennial for the tropical or subtropical garden, combining with other shade-tolerant South African species. A few specimens in a shaded border create an unmistakably tropical, bold statement in the spring garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; blood lily flowers appear in late summer to early autumn BEFORE the leaves emerge (the leaves follow and are present through winter and spring), then the plant goes summer dormant and leafless — a hysteranthous (flowers before leaves) flowering pattern unique within its family. Keep completely dry in summer. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs (neck exposed) in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade; blood lily is a botanical curiosity as much as a garden beauty — a South African Cape bulb that flowers before its leaves appear, sending up in late summer and early autumn (August–October) a fat, stout, dark-red to spotted stem only 4–8 inches tall topped by a dense, hemispherical to globular brush of hundreds of tiny, brilliant scarlet-red flowers with long, projecting red stamens — creating the unmistakable silhouette of a bright-red shaving brush of very large dimensions, as thick and dense as a pom-pom. The flower head remains decorative for several weeks. After flowering, two (rarely three) large, broad, thick, tongue-like, glossy-green leaves emerge and persist through winter and spring before dying back in summer dormancy. The dramatic, shaving-brush flower form — wholly unlike any other bulb flower — is immediately recognizable and memorable. Native to the rocky, sandy, dry slopes and scrubland of the Western Cape, it thrives in the Mediterranean-climate conditions of zones 9–11 in the garden. In humid-summer climates container culture with controlled summer dryness is recommended.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Spanish oyster plant requires a long, cool growing season (cool fall and winter for root development) before roots are harvested in late spring. The roots become milky-white and soft when cooked and have a mild, oyster-like or artichoke-like flavor. The stems and leaves are covered with strong spines — handle with care.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Spanish oyster plant is a Mediterranean native chicory-family perennial that has been grown as an edible root vegetable since at least medieval times throughout Spain, Italy, and southern France, where its long, white, deeply growing taproot is harvested in late spring and cooked like salsify or parsnip — boiled, roasted, or sautéed in olive oil. The cooked root has a pleasant, mild, artichoke-like or oyster-like flavor that makes it an interesting, underused culinary vegetable. The plant in the garden is a bold, deep-green, heavily spined thistle-like perennial with golden-yellow, star-shaped composite flowers in summer that are attractive to pollinators. The spines on stems and leaf margins are sharp and require careful handling. A drought-tolerant, deep-rooted perennial for Mediterranean-climate and Gulf Coast gardens where its cool-season root-development period suits the mild winter conditions. Once harvested, it is generally treated as an annual or biennial. Grown in Spain and southern Europe as an artisan market vegetable.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hairy urospermum self-sows freely in lean, dry soils and can naturalize aggressively in favorable Mediterranean-climate conditions. It thrives in poor, dry, rocky soils and declines in rich, moist conditions. The flowers are short-lived individually but produced continuously through a long season.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hairy urospermum is a Mediterranean coastal native of the chicory family producing a low rosette of deeply toothed, somewhat hairy, dandelion-like leaves and from spring through summer, a prolific and extended succession of substantial, clear, pale-primrose-yellow chicory-type flowers (1.5–2 inches across) on slender, branching stems. The flowers are produced continuously over several months rather than in a brief flush, and the clear, soft-yellow ray flowers with their slightly squared, five-notched tips have a clean, soft, naturalistic charm reminiscent of dandelion flowers but more refined in scale and arrangement. It thrives in poor, dry, rocky, and limestone soils of Mediterranean coastal scrub and roadsides — conditions that defeat most ornamentals — and is therefore useful in difficult, lean, dry positions in gardens in Mediterranean-climate zones. It self-sows freely in lean, open ground, building naturalized colonies that require no maintenance in appropriate conditions. An underused ornamental for gravel gardens, dry rocky banks, and naturalistic wildflower areas in zones 7–10.
